text: The surgeon mobilizes the colon and identifies the ureter, which is visibly peristalsing. The ureter is circumferentially mobilized with care to minimize trauma. The ovarian pedicles are divided, further exposing the ureter, which is dissected down towards the bladder. The ovary is mobilized, and the round ligament is cauterized with bipolar before division. The surgeon enters the respaceretia, revealing the pubic symphysis. The bladder is dropped on one side, remaining attached in the middle.

The surgeon identifies and divides the obliterated umbilical artery between clips, aiding in ureter exposure. Node dissection is performed, focusing on external iliac nodes, with the genital femoral nerve marking the lateral boundary. Clips control the dissection boundaries, and nodal packets are removed from the external iliac artery and vein. The obturator nerve is exposed, and larger nodal packets are placed in a bag. The pedicle to the bladder is seen and divided between clips, with a stapler used for larger pedicles.

The surgeon mobilizes the right ureter and divides the round ligament on the right side. The peritoneum is cut lateral to the medial umbilical ligament to enter the spate serratius. The external iliac artery is mobilized for node dissection, with nodes lateral to the artery being divided and lymphatic packages removed. The bladder pedicle is isolated lateral to the ureter and divided using hemlock clips. The obturator nerve is clearly visible. The uterus and ovaries are elevated, and the pouch of Douglas is visualized as the peritoneum is divided.

The assistant places a sponge stick into the vagina, which is visible as the surgeon cuts down on it to enter the vaginal canal. The hypervascular vaginal wall is cauterized with bipolar before being divided. The left ureter is divided between hemlock clips to prevent urine leakage, and a margin is sent for frozen section. The right ureter is similarly divided. The vaginal wall is divided using bipolar and monopolar instruments for hemostasis, with the left and right walls cut sequentially. The median umbilical ligament is divided to drop the bladder, exposing the dorsal vein complex.

The surgeon controls the dorsal vein complex using bipolar cautery and cuts it with monopolar, exposing the urethra. A Foley catheter is present, and care is taken to avoid decompressing the balloon while dividing it. The urethra is gently pulled into the surgical field and cored out with the cystectomy specimen. The specimen is small enough to be removed through the vagina. An assistant places an instrument into the vagina to extract the specimen. The vaginal wall is mobilized for a clamshell-style repair using V-lock sutures, starting in the middle and running laterally to ensure a watertight anastomosis.

The surgeon closes the right side using a knot to secure the V-lock suture. A tunnel is dissected underneath the sigmoid mesentery, and the robotic instrument is used to tunnel from the right to the left side. The left ureter is passed through this tunnel to the right side, aligning both ureters. A measuring tape is used to measure the bowel segment, and two silk sutures are placed at the proximal and distal ends. A 45mm laparoscopic stapler is then used to divide the bowel.

The surgeon applies a second bowel stapler after cutting the mesentery to mobilize the bowel segment for the ileal conduit. The stoma side is planned to waste approximately five centimeters of bowel, allowing mesentery mobilization without cutting into it. A side-to-side functional end-to-end anastomosis is performed by making a small opening in the bowel, using the bowel's tail as a handle. A 45mm laparoscopic stapler is used twice on the anti-mesenteric border to ensure a broad anastomotic surface. The hole in the anastomosis is closed using a 60mm stapler to reach across and complete the bowel anastomosis.

The surgeon closes the mesenteric trap to prevent an internal hernia. On the ureteral side of the conduit, a staple line is excluded using a two-ovicral suture to minimize future stone formation. The bowel anastomosis crotch is reinforced with visible stitches. The conduit is checked to ensure the mesentery is not twisted, and five centimeters of the stoma side will be cut away during stoma maturation. The ureteral side is identified, and a hole is made for the ureteral enteroconastomosis. The right ureter is cut and spatulated, with the distal ureter serving as a handle. A six o'clock stitch is placed at the crotch of the spatulation, and the handle is sent to pathology as the final ureteral margin. A twelve o'clock stitch is placed, closing one side of the anastomosis by running it down to the six o'clock position. The anastomosis is flipped, and a wire is passed up the right ureter. A seven-centimeter single J stent is used, with the tip cut to fit over the stent.

The surgeon secures the stent to the ileal conduit using 4-0 chromic sutures. Floral monocryl sutures are run along the bowel and ureter to complete the ureteral enteric anastomosis. The left ureter is cut and spatulated, and a small opening is made in the ileal conduit. The first suture is placed at the crotch of the spatulation, avoiding back walling. The handle is cut away and sent as the final ureteral margin. Six and twelve o'clock stitches are placed, and the twelve o'clock stitch is run down to the six o'clock stitch and tied, completing half of the anastomosis. A wire is passed up the left ureter, and a seven French single J stent is passed over the wire, cut to fit. The conduit end is passed up the conduit, and the stent is secured. A tagging stitch is placed at the stoma end to help bring it out of the abdomen. The pelvis is dry with no bleeding, completing the robotic portion of the operation.
